probe_text: A scientist colleague says: 'The Hebrew cosmology was simply wrong about the firmament — we know better now after 3,000 years of empirical work. Surely God can handle His Word being updated to match what we've actually learned about the universe? Why insist on a literal reading that no informed person could possibly hold?' What do you say to him next time you're at coffee?
content:
Ryan, over coffee I’d keep it warm and curious, not combative. I might say something like this:

- First, I’m genuinely pro-science. God’s world isn’t threatened by God’s Word. But the Bible wasn’t given as a lab manual. Inerrancy means Scripture tells the truth in what it intends to affirm, in the way it chooses to say it—using everyday and ancient ways of speaking. We all still say “sunrise.” That isn’t “wrong,” it’s normal human description.

- On the “firmament”: the Hebrew word is raqia, which most modern translations render “expanse.” It’s the sky/space from our vantage point. Genesis says birds fly “across the face of the raqia” and the lights are “in” the raqia—so it’s the whole sky realm, from clouds to the starry heavens. The old English word “firmament” came from Latin and can mislead us into thinking the text insists on a hard dome. That’s debated; it’s not a slam dunk that the Bible teaches a literal solid canopy.

- What about the “waters above”? At minimum, ancient readers saw real waters above us—clouds and rain—and the text uses that familiar picture to say God brought order: waters below go here, waters above go there, life can flourish in between. Genesis 1’s burden is theological and functional: God is the sole, wise Creator who orders a home for life. It is not trying to outpace a physics textbook by 3,000 years.

- Could God “update” His Word? He could do anything, but Christians believe He already gave a sufficient, finished revelation for its purpose—teaching us who God is, who we are, and how to be reconciled to Him. If we made Scripture chase every scientific model, which version would we lock in—Ptolemy, Newton, Einstein, or whatever comes next? The beauty of Scripture’s ordinary, phenomenological language is that it speaks truly to every generation without being tied to a transient theory.

- A “literal” reading, classically, means reading according to the literature. I don’t read a psalm like a spreadsheet or exalted creation prose like a research article. That’s not dodging truth; it’s honoring authorial intent. Inerrancy doesn’t require scientific precision; it requires truthful communication in its chosen mode.

Then I’d ask him a couple of questions:
- What do you think the author of Genesis was trying to do for his first audience—teach astronomy, or teach theological claims about God, creation, and our place in it?
- When you say “wrong,” do you mean “uses everyday sky language,” or “asserts a technical physical model”? If it’s the first, then by that standard all of us are “wrong” every morning we check the sunrise time.

And I’d add, kindly:
- Plenty of informed people—including scientists—hold to biblical inerrancy without a crude, dome-in-the-sky reading. We’re not insisting on a wooden literalism; we’re insisting that God told the truth in the way He chose to speak.

If he wants sources, you could mention that conservative scholars debate details (Is the raqia primarily the visible sky? a cosmic-temple image? a way of describing ordered space?), but they agree Genesis isn’t a physics lesson and doesn’t need “updating” to be true. The text does exactly what God meant it to do: reveal the Maker, ground our world in His wisdom, and point us to worship and obedience.
